query: How have party positions on asylum policy shifted since the 2023 election?
final_response:
Since the November 2023 election, party positions on asylum policy have shifted most notably among the right‑wing coalition partners, who struggled to deliver promised legislation. The VVD moved from celebrating the EU Asylum Pact and opposing the Distribution Act to a pragmatic stance that admitted past failures: “We were simply overrun for years, thanks partly to the ChristenUnie but also the VVD”^4. By spring 2026 the VVD accepted the Distribution Act could not be easily withdrawn and focused on reducing the existing Syrian asylum‑seeker stock^7. The PVV entered government as the self‑described “hard” asylum party but was later confronted by D66 with the coalition’s own record of “impossibly little asylum policy”^5. The PVV’s rhetoric remained radical—still calling for a full asylum stop and external processing “outside Europe and not at our expense”^7—yet its tone shifted from opposition firebrand to governing insider struggling to explain limited output.

The CDA underwent a notable realignment. Previously the architect of the two‑status system, it co‑signed a motion with GroenLinks‑PvdA on family reunification in mid‑2025^5, and by April 2026 it argued that the Distribution Act “removes every incentive to achieve real inflow reduction” while still insisting border municipalities must not be left to carry the burden^6. In contrast, D66 and GroenLinks‑PvdA held firm in their support for the Distribution Act and EU cooperation, D66 calling the plan to scrap the law “the biggest possible nonsense”^3.

Among smaller parties, the SP adopted a noticeably hardened posture, arguing that “the people’s will cannot be stopped by lawsuits”^4. JA21, after initially supporting the two‑status idea, later called Dutch asylum policy “completely bankrupt” and focused on external return hubs^6. SGP and FVD maintained radical‑right positions, with FVD describing the asylum crisis as “one of the biggest crises since the Second World War” and demanding a nexit^6^7. ChristenUnie consistently pressed for implementation of the EU Pact as the only realistic path^6. NSC took an evidence‑based line, only backing forced returns to Syria after a new country report^5. BBB, as a coalition partner, warned against coercing municipalities under the Distribution Act and demanded more local control^7.

Overall, the gap between campaign promises and governing reality reshaped positions among the right‑wing parties, while left‑wing and pro‑EU parties stayed the course.

## Sources
^1 Report of a committee debate on Alien and Asylum Policy, 20 December 2023, 2024‑01‑25 [2023D51631]
^2 Alien and Asylum Policy, two‑minute debate following committee debate of 12 September 2024, 2024‑09‑25 [2024D44246]
^3 Report of a committee debate on the JHA Council of 12‑13 December 2024, 2025‑01‑20 [2024D50542]
^4 Report of a committee debate on the JHA Council of 12‑13 June 2025 in Luxembourg, 2025‑07‑03 [2025D28547]
^5 Report of a committee debate on Alien and Asylum Policy, 18 June 2025, 2025‑07‑09 [2025D29495]
^6 Draft report on the second cycle of the Distribution Act, 2026‑04‑23 [2026D20721]
^7 Alien and Asylum Policy, 2026‑06‑22 [2026D31343]
political_response:
Since the November 2023 election, Dutch asylum policy debate has been shaped by a right‑wing coalition (PVV, VVD, NSC, BBB) that promised to deliver “the strictest asylum policy ever.” In practice, much of the promised legislation stalled, the coalition collapsed in mid‑2025, and party positions have shifted markedly as they grappled with the gap between campaign promises and governing reality.

### VVD: from celebratory pact‑maker to frustrated pragmatist
In December 2023, the VVD welcomed the EU Asylum and Migration Pact as a major breakthrough, while making clear it opposed the national Distribution Act (Spreidingswet) that compels municipalities to host asylum‑seekers ([2023D51631] 2024‑01‑25). By September 2024, the party linked scrapping the Distribution Act to a reduction in inflows, saying “we’ve made that coupling” ([2024D44246] 2024‑09‑25).

Once in government, the VVD acknowledged its own past responsibility: “We were simply overrun for years, thanks partly to the ChristenUnie but also the VVD … so the fact that a PVV minister is now, at least on paper, working on a stricter asylum policy is long overdue” ([2024D50542] 2025‑01‑20). In mid‑2025 the party pushed for ankle bracelets for asylum‑seekers who misbehave and demanded swift implementation of the EU Pact ([2025D28547] 2025‑07‑03). By spring 2026, however, the VVD appeared to accept that the Distribution Act could not be easily withdrawn and instead focused on reducing the existing stock of 36 000 Syrian asylum‑seekers, arguing “the thing exists … but if you work on that existing stock … then you can hopefully …” ([2026D31343] 2026‑06‑22). So the VVD moved from opposition to the Spreidingswet and a tone of principled firmness, to a more defensive stance that had to work within the constraints of a coalition that could not deliver its own promised laws.

### PVV: from opposition firebrand to governing party struggling to deliver
Before the election the PVV campaigned for closed borders and a complete asylum stop. After joining the coalition, it repeatedly presented itself as the only “hard” asylum party. In January 2025 a PVV speaker stressed that “it is high time” a PVV minister was working on stricter policy ([2024D50542] 2025‑01‑20). A few months later, the party accused the VVD of abandoning asylum as a theme: “the VVD wants to bring even more asylum‑seekers to the Netherlands by cooperating with GroenLinks‑PvdA”; “only the PVV still stands for a hard asylum policy” ([2025D29495] 2025‑07‑09). In mid‑2026 the PVV reiterated its ten‑point plan (including a full asylum stop and scrapping the Distribution Act) and said it would accept external processing only if it were “outside Europe and not at our expense” ([2026D31343] 2026‑06‑22). Nevertheless, the PVV’s tone shifted from pure opposition to that of an insider who has to explain why the coalition’s output – in its own words – is “impossible to have less asylum policy than this cabinet has delivered” (D66’s summary of the government’s record) ([2025D28547] 2025‑07‑03).

### CDA: from the architect of the two‑status system to cross‑bench motions with the left
In December 2023 the CDA stated clearly that “the Distribution Act concerns asylum‑seekers who are already here,” while inflows must be reduced ([2023D51631] 2024‑01‑25). The same party had earlier (in 2023) proposed the two‑status system that the VVD later embraced, but ChristenUnie’s Ceder pointed out that CDA and VVD had been warned the plan would fail because of insufficient capacity ([2024D50542] 2025‑01‑20). By mid‑2025, the CDA co‑signed a motion with GroenLinks‑PvdA on family‑reunification rules, distancing itself from the strict line of its former coalition partners ([2025D28547] 2025‑07‑03). In April 2026, CDA spokesperson Straatman argued that “the Distribution Act actually makes the problem worse, because it removes every incentive to achieve real inflow reduction” and announced the party wanted to withdraw the law – while still insisting that a “fair” country would not leave border municipalities like Ter Apel to carry the full burden ([2026D20721] 2026‑04‑23). The CDA thus moved from being a constructor of restrictive‑but‑pragmatic policies to a party that now demands the repeal of the flagship spreading law and cooperates with left‑wing parties on specific humanitarian motions.

### D66 and GroenLinks-PvdA: consistent defenders of the Distribution Act and EU cooperation
D66 and GroenLinks‑PvdA have not changed their core stance. Both consistently supported the Distribution Act and the EU Migration Pact. D66 called the VVD’s plan to scrap the law “the biggest possible nonsense” ([2024D44246] 2024‑09‑25) and later mocked the coalition for delivering “impossibly little asylum policy” ([2025D28547] 2025‑07‑03). GroenLinks‑PvdA continued to support the Spreidingswet, pointing out that no majority existed to repeal it ([2026D20721] 2026‑04‑23). The novelty is that GroenLinks‑PvdA found a tactical partner in the CDA for certain migration‑related motions, but that does not imply a shift in its own position.

### Smaller parties: radical continuity, local nuance, and one notable realignment
- **ChristenUnie** welcomed the EU Pact early on but repeatedly warned that restrictive ideas such as the two‑status system would collapse under administrative overload; it kept pressing for implementation of the Pact as the only realistic path ([2023D51631] 2024‑01‑25; [2024D50542] 2025‑01‑20; [2026D20721] 2026‑04‑23).
- **SGP** and **FVD** maintained their radical‑right profiles: SGP opposed the Distribution Act because of coercion of municipalities, demanded tougher border controls, and promoted return hubs; FVD called the asylum crisis “one of the biggest crises since the Second World War” and insisted only a “nexit” could solve it ([2025D29495] 2025‑07‑09; [2026D31343] 2026‑06‑22).
- **SP** adopted a posture that aligned with public calls for a “very strict asylum and migration policy,” arguing that “the people’s will cannot be stopped by lawsuits” ([2024D50542] 2025‑01‑20). This language represented a noticeable hardening compared with earlier, more nuanced SP statements.
- **JA21** initially worked with the two‑status idea that CDA and VVD championed, but was later challenged on why it had “gone along with it for a year and a half” ([2024D50542] 2025‑01‑20). By the summer of 2025, JA21 was entirely focused on external return hubs and called Dutch asylum policy “completely bankrupt” ([2026D20721] 2026‑04‑23).
- **NSC** took a cautious, evidence‑based line, refusing to support forced returns to Syria until a new country report indicated that the situation had improved ([2025D28547] 2025‑07‑03). When the report came, the party adjusted its position. In the 2026 debates, NSC was largely absent from the excerpts.
- **BBB**, as a coalition partner, warned that the Distribution Act would force municipalities to host centres against their will, and demanded more local control ([2026D31343] 2026‑06‑22).

### Overall pattern
Since the 2023 election, the parties that promised a tough asylum policy have struggled to deliver legislative results, producing internal tensions and a coalition breakdown. The VVD shifted from enthusiastic pact‑supporter to a pragmatic office‑holder that openly admitted its own past failures; the CDA moved from co‑designer of the two‑status system to a critic of the Spreidingswet and a partner of the left on specific compassion‑driven motions; and the PVV, though still rhetorically radical, was forced to justify a government it itself described as doing “nothing.” Meanwhile, D66, GroenLinks‑PvdA and ChristenUnie held firm to their pro‑European, law‑based lines, and JA21, SGP and FVD radicalised their demands. The debate thus shows not a single shift but a re‑alignment around the question of whether the state can actually deliver the “strictest asylum policy ever” that the 2023 election seemed to demand.

### Sources
- [2023D51631] 2024‑01‑25 – Report of a committee debate on Alien and Asylum Policy, held 20 December 2023 (*Verslag van een commissiedebat over Vreemdelingen- en asielbeleid, 20 december 2023*).
- [2024D44246] 2024‑09‑25 – Alien and Asylum Policy (*Vreemdelingen- en asielbeleid*), two‑minute debate following the committee debate of 12 September 2024.
- [2024D50542] 2025‑01‑20 – Report of a committee debate on the JHA Council of 12‑13 December 2024 (*Verslag van een commissiedebat over de JBZ-Raad van 12 en 13 december 2024*).
- [2025D28547] 2025‑07‑03 – Report of a committee debate on the JHA Council of 12‑13 June 2025 in Luxembourg (*Verslag van een commissiedebat over JBZ-Raad 12 – 13 juni 2025 te Luxemburg*).
- [2025D29495] 2025‑07‑09 – Report of a committee debate on Alien and Asylum Policy, held 18 June 2025 (*Verslag van een commissiedebat over Vreemdelingen- en asielbeleid*).
- [2026D20721] 2026‑04‑23 – Draft report on the second cycle of the Distribution Act (*Conceptverslag De tweede cyclus van de Spreidingswet*).
- [2026D31343] 2026‑06‑22 – Alien and Asylum Policy (*Vreemdelingen- en asielbeleid*).
